title: Expressions and evaluation

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Expressions and evaluation; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Expressions and evaluation
sources.claim: An expression combines operands and operators to produce a value; evaluation follows declared grouping, precedence, short-circuit, and type rules while creating intermediate results.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An expression combines operands and operators to produce a value; evaluation follows declared grouping, precedence, short-circuit, and type rules while creating intermediate results.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The expression 2 + 3 * 4 evaluates multiplication first to 12 and then addition to 14, while parentheses can request a different grouping. Your goal is to evaluate mixed arithmetic, comparison and Boolean expressions step by step. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pseudocode; Variables and assignment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: An expression combines operands and operators to produce a value; evaluation follows declared grouping, precedence, short-circuit, and type rules while creating intermediate results. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Precedence varies by operator and language, floating-point arithmetic is not exact real arithmetic, and side effects inside expressions can make order important or unsafe. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Expressions are always evaluated strictly from left to right, regardless of parentheses or operator precedence.

includes: Evaluate operations using operands, precedence and intermediate results.
Evaluate mixed arithmetic, comparison and Boolean expressions step by step.
excludes: Precedence varies by operator and language, floating-point arithmetic is not exact real arithmetic, and side effects inside expressions can make order important or unsafe.

outcomes: Evaluate mixed arithmetic, comparison and Boolean expressions step by step.

examples.title: Expressions and evaluation: direct case
examples.situation: The expression 2 + 3 * 4 evaluates multiplication first to 12 and then addition to 14, while parentheses can request a different grouping.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: The expression 2 + 3 * 4 evaluates multiplication first to 12 and then addition to 14, while parentheses can request a different grouping.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Trace the syntax tree or explicit grouping, evaluate inner and higher-precedence operations first, and apply the runtime's documented order and type rules.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Trace the syntax tree or explicit grouping, evaluate inner and higher-precedence operations first, and apply the runtime's documented order and type rules.

examples.title: Expressions and evaluation: boundary case
examples.situation: The Boolean expression hasTicket AND age >= 18 can stop after a false hasTicket in a language with short-circuit conjunction.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: The Boolean expression hasTicket AND age >= 18 can stop after a false hasTicket in a language with short-circuit conjunction.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Precedence varies by operator and language, floating-point arithmetic is not exact real arithmetic, and side effects inside expressions can make order important or unsafe.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Expressions are always evaluated strictly from left to right, regardless of parentheses or operator precedence.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Evaluate mixed arithmetic, comparison and Boolean expressions step by step.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Expressions are always evaluated strictly from left to right, regardless of parentheses or operator precedence.
misconceptions.correction: Trace the syntax tree or explicit grouping, evaluate inner and higher-precedence operations first, and apply the runtime's documented order and type rules.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Expressions are always evaluated strictly from left to right, regardless of parentheses or operator precedence.

analogy.example: A labeled scoreboard can model Expressions and evaluation: each label exposes a current value and each recorded event produces the next visible state.
usefulFor: Reasoning about values, expressions, named state, and changes across time
limitations: The scoreboard does not accurately model binary representation, scope, aliases, object identity, or language coercion.

recap.summary: You should now be able to evaluate mixed arithmetic, comparison and Boolean expressions step by step. Keep this boundary in view: Precedence varies by operator and language, floating-point arithmetic is not exact real arithmetic, and side effects inside expressions can make order important or unsafe.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Expressions and evaluation without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Expressions are always evaluated strictly from left to right, regardless of parentheses or operator precedence.
Apply the model to this case from memory: The Boolean expression hasTicket AND age >= 18 can stop after a false hasTicket in a language with short-circuit conjunction.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Mutation and aliases”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
